Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Interface IRefreshTicker

Namespace
Pandowdy.UI.Interfaces
Assembly
Pandowdy.UI.dll

Provides a 60 Hz refresh signal for driving UI-based frame rendering and updates.

public interface IRefreshTicker

Remarks

This interface represents a timing source that emits periodic signals at approximately 60 Hz (60 times per second), matching the typical video refresh rate of the Apple IIe and modern displays. It's designed to be implemented by UI-layer components (such as Avalonia's DispatcherTimer) to drive frame rendering in a way that's synchronized with the UI thread and display refresh.

The ticker uses the Observable pattern (Rx.NET) to provide a reactive stream of timestamps. Subscribers can use this stream to trigger frame generation, display updates, and other time-based operations that need to occur at regular intervals.

Implementation Note: This interface is implemented in the UI layer using platform-specific timing mechanisms (DispatcherTimer, animation frames, etc.) that are only available in the UI context.

Properties

Stream

Gets an observable stream that emits timestamps at approximately 60 Hz.

IObservable<DateTime> Stream { get; }

Property Value

IObservable<DateTime>

An IObservable<T> that emits the current timestamp each time the ticker fires. Subscribers can observe this stream to receive periodic notifications for driving frame updates.

Remarks

The stream emits approximately 60 times per second when the ticker is running. Each emission provides the current DateTime at the time of the tick, which can be used for time-based calculations or simply as a signal to perform periodic work.

Typical usage pattern: 

ticker.Stream
    .ObserveOn(RxApp.MainThreadScheduler)
    .Subscribe(_ => RenderFrame());

The actual timing may vary slightly based on system load and UI thread scheduling, but should maintain an average rate close to 60 Hz for smooth animation.

Methods

Start()

Starts the refresh ticker, causing it to begin emitting periodic signals.

void Start()

Remarks

After calling this method, the Stream will begin emitting timestamps at approximately 60 Hz. This method is typically called when the emulator starts running or when the UI becomes visible and ready to display frames.

Calling Start() on an already-running ticker should be safe (idempotent).

Stop()

Stops the refresh ticker, causing it to cease emitting signals.

void Stop()

Remarks

After calling this method, the Stream will stop emitting timestamps until Start() is called again. This method is typically called when the emulator is paused or when the UI is no longer visible.

Calling Stop() on an already-stopped ticker should be safe (idempotent).
